title: Osteocyte-Intrinsic MMP13 Exacerbates Injury-Induced Temporomandibular Joint Osteoarthritis
research_question: Does osteocytic MMP13 play a causal role in temporomandibular joint osteoarthritis (TMJOA) progression, similar to its role in knee OA?
methods_summary: Generated DMP1-cre+/-;MMP13fl/fl mice with osteocyte-intrinsic MMP13 deficiency
Induced TMJOA via intra-articular monosodium iodoacetate (MIA) injection in 12-week-old male mice
Evaluated TMJ cartilage degeneration using Modified TMJ Mankin scoring on Safranin-O/Fast Green stained sections
Assessed subchondral bone lacunocanalicular network (LCN) via Ploton silver nitrate stain
Analyzed collagen fiber orientation using picrosirius red stain and polarized light microscopy
Measured bone resorption via TRAP stain and cathepsin K immunohistochemistry
Performed microCT analysis of subchondral bone parameters
Compared TMJ findings with knee OA using meniscal-ligamentous injury (MLI) model
dataset: 16-week-old male DMP1-cre+/-;MMP13fl/fl mice and Cre-negative littermate controls (mixed FVB and C57BL/6 background); n=6-9 per group for TMJ studies, n=6-11 per group for knee studies
key_findings: Osteocytic MMP13 deficiency protects TMJ from MIA-induced cartilage degeneration and subchondral bone changes
MIA-treated control mice showed increased Modified TMJ Mankin scores and osteocyte lacunar density, while DMP1-cre+/-;MMP13fl/fl mice were protected
Collagen organization in TMJ subchondral bone was unaffected by genotype or MIA treatment
TRAP staining showed a significant genotype-treatment interaction effect on bone resorption
MicroCT revealed no significant changes in TMJ subchondral bone mineralization across groups
In knee joints, osteocytic MMP13 deficiency protected against injury-induced subchondral bone plate thickening
Baseline TMJ phenotype in DMP1-cre+/-;MMP13fl/fl mice was normal, unlike knee joints which showed baseline OA
extracted_insights: Osteocytic MMP13 plays a joint-specific role: it is not required for TMJ homeostasis at baseline but is necessary for injury-induced TMJOA progression. Deficiency of osteocytic MMP13 protects both TMJ and knee from injury-dependent subchondral bone changes, despite opposite effects on baseline cartilage degeneration in the two joints.
